1→# Contributing to Azure MCP
     2→
     3→There are many ways to contribute to the Azure MCP project: reporting bugs, submitting pull requests, and creating suggestions.
     4→After cloning and building the repo, check out the [GitHub project](https://github.com/orgs/Azure/projects/812/views/13) and [issues list](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/issues). Issues labeled [help wanted](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/labels/help%20wanted) are good issues to submit a PR for. Issues labeled [good first issue](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/labels/good%20first%20issue) are great candidates to pick up if you are in the code for the first time.
     5→
     6→>[!IMPORTANT]
     7→If you are contributing significant changes, or if the issue is already assigned to a specific milestone, please discuss with the assignee of the issue first before starting to work on the issue.
     8
     9→## Table of Contents

    34→  - [Questions and Support](#questions-and-support)

    38→
    39→## Getting Started
    40→
    41→> ⚠️ If you are a Microsoft employee then please also review our [Azure Internal Onboarding Documentation](https://aka.ms/azmcp/intake) for getting setup
    42→
    43→### Prerequisites
    44→
    45→1. **VS Code**: Install either [stable](https://code.visualstudio.com/download) or [Insiders](https://code.visualstudio.com/insiders) release
    46→2. **GitHub Copilot**: Install [GitHub Copilot](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=GitHub.copilot) and [GitHub Copilot Chat](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=GitHub.copilot-chat) extensions
    47→3. **Node.js**: Install [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/en/download) 20 or later (ensure `node` and `npm` are in your PATH)
    48→4. **PowerShell**: Install [PowerShell](https://learn.microsoft.com/powershell/scripting/install/installing-powershell) 7.0 or later (required for build and test scripts)
    49→
    50→### Project Structure
    51→
    52→The project is organized as follows:
    53→- `src/` - Main source code
    54→  - `Areas/{Area}/` - Service specific code
    55→    - `Commands/` - Command implementations
    56→    - `Models/` - Service specific models
    57→    - `Services/` - Service implementations and interfaces
    58→    - `Options/` - Service specific command options
    59→  - `Commands/` - Command base and helper classes
    60→  - `Models/` - Common models and base classes
    61→  - `Services/` - Common services
    62→  - `Options/` - Command option definitions
    63→- `tests/` - Test files
    64→  - `Areas/{Area}/` - Service specific tests
    65→    - `UnitTests/` - Unit tests require no authentication or test resources
    66→    - `LiveTests/` - Live tests depend on Azure resources and authentication
    67→- `docs/` - Documentation
    68→
    69→## Development Workflow
    70→
    71→### Development Process
    72→
    73→1. Fork the repository
    74→2. Create a feature branch
    75→3. Make your changes
    76→4. Write or update tests
    77→5. Test locally
    78→6. Submit a pull request
    79→
    80→### Adding a New Command
    81→
    82→1. **Create an issue** with title: "Add command: azmcp [namespace] [resource] [operation]" and detailed description
    83→
    84→2. **Set up development environment**:
    85→   - Open VS Code Insiders
    86→   - Open the Copilot Chat view
    87→   - Select "Agent" mode
    88→
    89→3. **Generate the command** using Copilot:
    90→   ```
    91→   Execute in Copilot Chat:
    92→   "create [namespace] [resource] [operation] command using #new-command.md as a reference"
    93→   ```
    94→
    95→4. **Follow implementation guidelines** in [src/Docs/new-command.md](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/blob/main/src/Docs/new-command.md)
    96→
    97→5. **Add documentation**:
    98→   - Update [/docs/azmcp-commands.md](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/blob/main/docs/azmcp-commands.md)
    99→   - Update [/e2eTests/e2eTestPrompts.md](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/blob/main/e2eTests/e2eTestPrompts.md)
   100→   - Update [README.md](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/blob/main/README.md)
   101→
   102→6. **Add CODEOWNERS entry** in [CODEOWNERS](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/blob/main/.github/CODEOWNERS) [(example)](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/commit/08f73efe826d5d47c0f93be5ed9e614740e82091)
   103→
   104→7. **Create Pull Request**:
   105→   - Reference the issue you created
   106→   - Include tests in the `/tests` folder
   107→   - Ensure all tests pass
   108→   - Add sample prompts to `/e2eTests/e2eTestPrompts.md`
   109→   - Follow code style requirements
   110→
   111→## Testing
   112→
   113→Command authors must provide both unit tests and end-to-end test prompts.
   114→
   115→### Unit Tests
   116→
   117→Unit tests live under the `/tests` folder. To run tests:
   118→
   119→```pwsh
   120→./eng/scripts/Test-Code.ps1
   121→```
   122→
   123→Requirements:
   124→- Each command should have unit tests
   125→- Tests should cover success and error scenarios
   126→- Mock external service calls
   127→- Test argument validation
   128→
   129→### End-to-end Tests
   130→
   131→End-to-end tests are performed manually. Command authors must thoroughly test each command to ensure correct tool invocation and results. At least one prompt per tool is required and should be added to `/e2eTests/e2eTestPrompts.md`.
   132→
   133→### Testing Local Build with VS Code
   134→
   135→To run the Azure MCP server from source for local development:
   136→
   137→#### Build the Server
   138→
   139→Navigate to the MCP server source directory and build the project:
   140→
   141→```bash
   142→dotnet build
   143→```
   144→
   145→#### Configure mcp.json
   146→
   147→Update your mcp.json to point to the locally built azmcp executable:
   148→
   149→```json
   150→{
   151→  "servers": {
   152→    "azure-mcp-server": {
   153→      "type": "stdio",
   154→      "command": "<absolute-path-to>/azure-mcp/src/bin/Debug/net9.0/azmcp[.exe]",
   155→      "args": ["server", "start"]
   156→    }
   157→  }
   158→}
   159→```
   160→
   161→> **Note:** Replace `<absolute-path-to>` with the full path to your built executable.
   162→> On **Windows**, use `azmcp.exe`. On **macOS/Linux**, use `azmcp`.
   163→
   164→#### Server Modes
   165→
   166→Optional `--namespace` and `--mode` parameters can be used to configure different server modes:
   167→
   168→**Default Mode** (no additional parameters):
   169→```json
   170→{
   171→  "servers": {
   172→    "azure-mcp-server": {
   173→      "type": "stdio",
   174→      "command": "<absolute-path-to>/azure-mcp/src/bin/Debug/net9.0/azmcp[.exe]",
   175→      "args": ["server", "start"]
   176→    }
   177→  }
   178→}
   179→```
   180→
   181→**Namespace Mode** (expose specific services):
   182→```json
   183→{
   184→  "servers": {
   185→    "azure-mcp-server": {
   186→      "type": "stdio",
   187→      "command": "<absolute-path-to>/azure-mcp/src/bin/Debug/net9.0/azmcp[.exe]",
   188→      "args": ["server", "start", "--namespace", "storage", "--namespace", "keyvault"]
   189→    }
   190→  }
   191→}
   192→```
   193→
   194→**Namespace Proxy Mode** (collapse tools by namespace):
   195→```json
   196→{
   197→  "servers": {
   198→    "azure-mcp-server": {
   199→      "type": "stdio",
   200→      "command": "<absolute-path-to>/azure-mcp/src/bin/Debug/net9.0/azmcp[.exe]",
   201→      "args": ["server", "start", "--mode", "namespace"]
   202→    }
   203→  }
   204→}
   205→```
   206→
   207→**Single Tool Proxy Mode** (single "azure" tool with internal routing):
   208→```json
   209→{
   210→  "servers": {
   211→    "azure-mcp-server": {
   212→      "type": "stdio",
   213→      "command": "<absolute-path-to>/azure-mcp/src/bin/Debug/net9.0/azmcp[.exe]",
   214→      "args": ["server", "start", "--mode", "single"]
   215→    }
   216→  }
   217→}
   218→```
   219→
   220→**Combined Mode** (filter namespaces with proxy mode):
   221→```json
   222→{
   223→  "servers": {
   224→    "azure-mcp-server": {
   225→      "type": "stdio",
   226→      "command": "<absolute-path-to>/azure-mcp/src/bin/Debug/net9.0/azmcp[.exe]",
   227→      "args": ["server", "start", "--namespace", "storage", "--namespace", "keyvault", "--mode", "namespace"]
   228→    }
   229→  }
   230→}
   231→```
   232→
   233→> **Server Mode Summary:**
   234→> - **Default Mode**: No additional parameters - exposes all tools individually
   235→> - **Namespace Mode**: `--namespace <service-name>` - expose specific services
   236→> - **Namespace Proxy Mode**: `--mode namespace` - collapse tools by namespace (useful for VS Code's 128 tool limit)
   237→> - **Single Tool Mode**: `--mode single` - single "azure" tool with internal routing
   238→> - **Combined Mode**: Both `--namespace` and `--mode` can be used together
   239→
   240→#### Start from IDE
   241→
   242→With the configuration in place, you can launch the MCP server directly from your IDE or any tooling that uses `mcp.json`.
   243→
   244→### Live Tests
   245→
   246→> ⚠️ If you are a Microsoft employee with Azure source permissions then please review our [Azure Internal Onboarding Documentation](https://aka.ms/azmcp/intake). Team members can run live tests by adding this comment to the PR `/azp run azure - mcp` to start the run.
   247→
   248→Before running live tests:
   249→- [Install Azure PowerShell](https://learn.microsoft.com/powershell/azure/install-azure-powershell)
   250→- [Install Azure Bicep](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-resource-manager/bicep/install#install-manually)
   251→- Login to Azure PowerShell: [`Connect-AzAccount`](https://learn.microsoft.com/powershell/azure/authenticate-interactive?view=azps-13.4.0)
   252→- Deploy test resources:
   253→
   254→```pwsh
   255→./eng/scripts/Deploy-TestResources.ps1
   256→```
   257→
   258→**Deploy-TestResources.ps1 Parameters:**
   259→
   260→| Parameter           | Type     | Description                                                                                                                   |
   261→|---------------------|----------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
   262→| `Areas`             | string[] | Reduce the scope of your deployment to specific areas. e.g. `-Areas Storage, KeyVault`                                       |
   263→| `SubscriptionId`    | string   | Deploy to a specific subscription, otherwise, for internal users, the subscription will be defaulted to a known subscription. |
   264→| `ResourceGroupName` | string   | Set the resource group name. Defaults to "{username}-mcp{hash(username)}".                                                    |
   265→| `BaseName`          | string   | Set the base name for all of the resources. Defaults to "mcp{hash}".                                                          |
   266→| `Unique`            | switch   | Make `{hash}` in the resource group name and base name unique per invocation. Defaults to a hash of your username             |
   267→| `DeleteAfterHours`  | int      | Change the timespan used to set the DeleteAfter tag. Defaults to 12 hours.                                                   |
   268→
   269→After deploying test resources, you should have a `.testsettings.json` file with your deployment information in the root of the repo.
   270→
   271→Run live tests with:
   272→```pwsh
   273→./eng/scripts/Test-Code.ps1 -Live
   274→```
   275→
   276→You can scope tests to specific areas:
   277→```pwsh
   278→./eng/scripts/Test-Code.ps1 -Live -Areas Storage, KeyVault
   279→```
   280→
   281→### NPX Live Tests
   282→
   283→You can set the `TestPackage` parameter in `.testsettings.json` to have live tests run `npx` targeting an arbitrary Azure MCP package:
   284→
   285→```json
   286→{
   287→  "TenantId": "a20062a8-ff76-41c2-8a6d-5e843da7b051",
   288→  "TenantName": "Your Tenant",
   289→  "SubscriptionId": "cd27afdc-9976-4f08-96e9-cad120a91560",
   290→  "SubscriptionName": "Your Subscription",
   291→  "ResourceGroupName": "rg-abcdefg",
   292→  "ResourceBaseName": "t1234567890",
   293→  "TestPackage": "@azure/mcp@0.0.10"
   294→}
   295→```
   296→
   297→To run live tests against the local build of an npm module:
   298→```pwsh
   299→./eng/scripts/Build-Local.ps1
   300→```
   301→
   302→This will produce .tgz files in the `.dist` directory and set the `TestPackage` parameter in the `.testsettings.json` file:
   303→
   304→```json
   305→"TestPackage": "file://D:\\repos\\azure-mcp\\.dist\\wrapper\\azure-mcp-0.0.12-alpha.1746488279.tgz"
   306→```
   307→
   308→### Debugging Live Tests
   309→
   310→This section assumes that the necessary Azure resources for live tests are already deployed and that the `.testsettings.json` file with deployment information is located at the root of the local repository clone.
   311→
   312→To debug the Azure MCP Server (`azmcp`) when running live tests in VS Code:
   313→
   314→1. Build the package with debug symbols: `./eng/scripts/Build-Local.ps1 -DebugBuild`
   315→2. Set a breakpoint in a command file (e.g., [`KeyValueListCommand.ExecuteAsync`](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/blob/4ed650a0507921273acc7b382a79049809ef39c1/src/Commands/AppConfig/KeyValue/KeyValueListCommand.cs#L48))
   316→3. In VS Code, navigate to a test method (e.g., [`AppConfigCommandTests::Should_list_appconfig_kvs()`](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/blob/4ed650a0507921273acc7b382a79049809ef39c1/tests/Client/AppConfigCommandTests.cs#L56)), add a breakpoint to `CallToolAsync` call in the test method, then right-click and select **Debug Test**
   317→4. Find the `azmcp` process ID:
   318→
   319→```shell
   320→pgrep -fl azmcp
   321→```
   322→
   323→```powershell
   324→Get-Process | Where-Object { $_.ProcessName -like "*azmcp*" } | Select-Object Id, ProcessName, Path
   325→```
   326→
   327→5. Open the Command Palette (`Cmd+Shift+P` on Mac, `Ctrl+Shift+P` on Windows/Linux), select **Debug: Attach to .NET 5+ or .NET Core process**, and enter the `azmcp` process ID
   328→6. Hit F5 to "Continue" debugging, the debugger should attach to `azmcp` and hit the breakpoint in command file
   329→
   330→## Quality and Standards
   331→
   332→### Code Style
   333→
   334→To ensure consistent code quality, code format checks will run during all PR and CI builds. Run `dotnet format` before submitting to catch format errors early.
   335→
   336→**Spelling Check**
   337→
   338→To ensure consistent spelling across the codebase, run the spelling check before submitting:
   339→
   340→```pwsh
   341→.\eng\common\spelling\Invoke-Cspell.ps1
   342→```
   343→
   344→This will check all files for spelling errors using the project's dictionary. Add any new technical terms or proper nouns to `.vscode/cspell.json` if needed.
   345→
   346→**Requirements:**
   347→- Follow C# coding conventions
   348→- No comments in implementation code (code should be self-documenting)
   349→- Use descriptive variable and method names
   350→- Follow the exact file structure and naming conventions
   351→- Use proper error handling patterns
   352→- XML documentation for public APIs
   353→- Follow Model Context Protocol (MCP) patterns
   354→
   355→### AOT Compatibility Analysis
   356→
   357→The AOT compatibility analysis helps identify potential issues that might prevent the Azure MCP Server from working correctly when compiled with AOT or when trimming is enabled.
   358→
   359→#### Running the Analysis
   360→
   361→To run the AOT compatibility analysis locally:
   362→
   363→```pwsh
   364→./eng/scripts/Analyze-AOT-Compact.ps1
   365→```
   366→
   367→The HTML report will be generated at `.work/aotCompactReport/aot-compact-report.html` and automatically opened in your default browser.
   368→
   369→To output the report to console, run the analysis with `-OutputFormat Console` argument.
   370→
   371→AOT compatibility warnings typically indicate:
   372→- Use of reflection without proper annotations
   373→- Serialization of types that might be trimmed
   374→- Dynamic code generation
   375→- Use of `RequiresUnreferencedCodeAttribute` methods without proper precautions
   376→
   377→#### Installing Git Hooks
   378→
   379→You can install our pre-push hook to catch code format issues by automatically running `dotnet format` before each `git push`:
   380→
   381→- `./eng/scripts/Install-GitHooks.ps1` - Installs the pre-push hook into your local repo
   382→- `./eng/scripts/Remove-GitHooks.ps1` - Disables any git hooks in your local repo
   383→
   384→### Model Context Protocol (MCP)
   385→
   386→The Azure MCP Server implements the [Model Context Protocol specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io). When adding new commands:
   387→
   388→- Follow MCP JSON schema patterns
   389→- Implement proper context handling
   390→- Use standardized response formats
   391→- Handle errors according to MCP specifications
   392→- Provide proper argument suggestions
   393→
   394→## Advanced Configuration
   395→
   396→### Configuring External MCP Servers
   397→
   398→The Azure MCP Server supports connecting to external MCP servers through an embedded `registry.json` configuration file. This enables the server to act as a proxy, aggregating tools from multiple MCP servers into a single interface. The registry follows the same configuration schema as VS Code's `mcp.json`.
   399→
   400→#### Registry Configuration
   401→
   402→External MCP servers are defined in the embedded resource file `src/Areas/Server/Resources/registry.json`. This file contains server configurations that support both SSE (Server-Sent Events) and stdio transport mechanisms, following the standard MCP configuration format.
   403→
   404→The registry structure follows this format:
   405→
   406→```json
   407→{
   408→  "servers": {
   409→    "documentation": {
   410→      "url": "https://learn.microsoft.com/api/mcp",
   411→      "description": "Search official Microsoft/Azure documentation..."
   412→    },
   413→    "another-server": {
   414→      "type": "stdio",
   415→      "command": "path/to/executable",
   416→      "args": ["arg1", "arg2"],
   417→      "env": {
   418→        "ENV_VAR": "value"
   419→      },
   420→      "description": "Another MCP server using stdio transport"
   421→    }
   422→  }
   423→}
   424→```
   425→
   426→#### Transport Types
   427→
   428→**SSE (Server-Sent Events) Transport:**
   429→- Use the `url` property to specify the endpoint
   430→- Supports HTTP-based communication with automatic transport mode detection
   431→- Best for web-based MCP servers and remote endpoints
   432→
   433→**Stdio Transport:**
   434→- Use `type: "stdio"` with the `command` property
   435→- Supports launching external processes that communicate via standard input/output
   436→- Use `args` array for command-line arguments
   437→- Use `env` object for environment variables
   438→- Best for local executables, command-line tools, and local MCP servers
   439→
   440→#### Server Discovery and Namespace Filtering
   441→
   442→External servers are automatically discovered when the Azure MCP Server starts. They can be filtered using the same namespace mechanisms as built-in commands:
   443→
   444→```bash
   445→# Include only specific external servers
   446→azmcp server start --namespace documentation --namespace another-server
   447→
   448→# Use namespace mode to group tools exposed by external servers
   449→azmcp server start --mode namespace
   450→```
   451→
   452→#### Adding New External MCP Servers
   453→
   454→To add a new external MCP server to the registry:
   455→
   456→1. Edit `src/Areas/Server/Resources/registry.json`
   457→2. Add your server configuration under the `servers` object using VS Code's MCP configuration schema
   458→3. Use a unique identifier as the key
   459→4. Provide either a `url` for SSE transport or `type: "stdio"` with `command` for stdio transport
   460→5. Include a descriptive `description` field
   461→6. Rebuild the project to embed the updated registry
   462→
   463→#### Example External Servers
   464→
   465→The current registry includes:
   466→- **documentation**: Microsoft Learn documentation search via SSE transport
   467→- Additional external servers can be added following the same pattern as VS Code's mcp.json
   468→
   469→External servers integrate seamlessly with the Azure MCP Server's tool aggregation, appearing alongside native Azure commands in the unified tool interface. This allows you to combine local MCP servers, remote MCP endpoints, and Azure-specific tools in a single interface.
   470→
   471→## Project Management
   472→
   473→### Pull Request Process
   474→
   475→1. Update documentation reflecting any changes
   476→2. Add or update tests as needed
   477→3. Reference the original issue
   478→4. Wait for review and address any feedback
   479→
   480→### Builds and Releases (Internal)
   481→
   482→The internal pipeline [azure-mcp](https://dev.azure.com/azure-sdk/internal/_build?definitionId=7571) is used for all official releases and CI builds. On every merge to main, a build will run and will produce a dynamically named prerelease package on the public dev feed, e.g. [@azure/mcp@0.0.10-beta.4799791](https://dev.azure.com/azure-sdk/public/_artifacts/feed/azure-sdk-for-js/Npm/@azure%2Fmcp/overview/0.0.10-beta.4799791).
   483→
   484→Only manual runs of the pipeline sign and publish packages. Building `main` or `hotfix/*` will publish to `npmjs.com`, all other refs will publish to the [public dev feed](https://dev.azure.com/azure-sdk/public/_artifacts/feed/azure-sdk-for-js).
   485→
   486→Packages published to npmjs.com will always use the `@latest` [dist-tag](https://docs.npmjs.com/downloading-and-installing-packages-locally#installing-a-package-with-dist-tags).
   487→
   488→Packages published to the dev feed will use:
   489→- `@latest` for the latest official/release build
   490→- `@dev` for the latest CI build of main
   491→- `@pre` for any arbitrary pipeline run or feature branch build
   492→
   493→#### PR Validation
   494→
   495→To run live tests for a PR, inspect the PR code for any suspicious changes, then add the comment `/azp run azure - mcp` to the pull request. This will queue a PR triggered run which will build, run unit tests, deploy test resources and run live tests.
   496→
   497→If you would like to see the product of a PR as a package on the dev feed, after thoroughly inspecting the change, create a branch in the main repo and manually trigger an [azure - mcp](https://dev.azure.com/azure-sdk/internal/_build?definitionId=7571) pipeline run against that branch. This will queue a manually triggered run which will build, run unit tests, deploy test resources, run live tests, sign and publish the packages to the dev feed.
   498→
   499→Instructions for consuming the package from the dev feed can be found in the "Extensions" tab of the pipeline run page.
   500→

# Contributing to Azure MCP

There are many ways to contribute to the Azure MCP project: reporting bugs, submitting pull requests, and creating suggestions.
After cloning and building the repo, check out the [GitHub project](https://github.com/orgs/Azure/projects/812/views/13) and [issues list](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/issues). Issues labeled [help wanted](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/labels/help%20wanted) are good issues to submit a PR for. Issues labeled [good first issue](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/labels/good%20first%20issue) are great candidates to pick up if you are in the code for the first time.

>[!IMPORTANT]
If you are contributing significant changes, or if the issue is already assigned to a specific milestone, please discuss with the assignee of the issue first before starting to work on the issue.

## Table of Contents

- [Getting Started](#getting-started)
  - [Prerequisites](#prerequisites)
  - [Project Structure](#project-structure)
- [Development Workflow](#development-workflow)
  - [Development Process](#development-process)
  - [Adding a New Command](#adding-a-new-command)
- [Testing](#testing)
  - [Unit Tests](#unit-tests)
  - [End-to-end Tests](#end-to-end-tests)
  - [Testing Local Build with VS Code](#testing-local-build-with-vs-code)
  - [Live Tests](#live-tests)
  - [NPX Live Tests](#npx-live-tests)
  - [Debugging Live Tests](#debugging-live-tests)
- [Quality and Standards](#quality-and-standards)
  - [Code Style](#code-style)
  - [AOT Compatibility Analysis](#aot-compatibility-analysis)
  - [Model Context Protocol (MCP)](#model-context-protocol-mcp)
- [Advanced Configuration](#advanced-configuration)
  - [Configuring External MCP Servers](#configuring-external-mcp-servers)
- [Project Management](#project-management)
  - [Pull Request Process](#pull-request-process)
  - [Builds and Releases (Internal)](#builds-and-releases-internal)
- [Support and Community](#support-and-community)
  - [Questions and Support](#questions-and-support)
  - [Additional Resources](#additional-resources)
  - [Code of Conduct](#code-of-conduct)
  - [License](#license)

## Getting Started

> ⚠️ If you are a Microsoft employee then please also review our [Azure Internal Onboarding Documentation](https://aka.ms/azmcp/intake) for getting setup

### Prerequisites

1. **VS Code**: Install either [stable](https://code.visualstudio.com/download) or [Insiders](https://code.visualstudio.com/insiders) release
2. **GitHub Copilot**: Install [GitHub Copilot](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=GitHub.copilot) and [GitHub Copilot Chat](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=GitHub.copilot-chat) extensions
3. **Node.js**: Install [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/en/download) 20 or later (ensure `node` and `npm` are in your PATH)
4. **PowerShell**: Install [PowerShell](https://learn.microsoft.com/powershell/scripting/install/installing-powershell) 7.0 or later (required for build and test scripts)

### Project Structure

The project is organized as follows:
- `src/` - Main source code
  - `Areas/{Area}/` - Service specific code
    - `Commands/` - Command implementations
    - `Models/` - Service specific models
    - `Services/` - Service implementations and interfaces
    - `Options/` - Service specific command options
  - `Commands/` - Command base and helper classes
  - `Models/` - Common models and base classes
  - `Services/` - Common services
  - `Options/` - Command option definitions
- `tests/` - Test files
  - `Areas/{Area}/` - Service specific tests
    - `UnitTests/` - Unit tests require no authentication or test resources
    - `LiveTests/` - Live tests depend on Azure resources and authentication
- `docs/` - Documentation

## Development Workflow

### Development Process

1. Fork the repository
2. Create a feature branch
3. Make your changes
4. Write or update tests
5. Test locally
6. Submit a pull request

### Adding a New Command

1. **Create an issue** with title: "Add command: azmcp [namespace] [resource] [operation]" and detailed description

2. **Set up development environment**:
   - Open VS Code Insiders
   - Open the Copilot Chat view
   - Select "Agent" mode

3. **Generate the command** using Copilot:
   ```
   Execute in Copilot Chat:
   "create [namespace] [resource] [operation] command using #new-command.md as a reference"
   ```

4. **Follow implementation guidelines** in [src/Docs/new-command.md](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/blob/main/src/Docs/new-command.md)

5. **Add documentation**:
   - Update [/docs/azmcp-commands.md](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/blob/main/docs/azmcp-commands.md)
   - Update [/e2eTests/e2eTestPrompts.md](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/blob/main/e2eTests/e2eTestPrompts.md)
   - Update [README.md](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/blob/main/README.md)

6. **Add CODEOWNERS entry** in [CODEOWNERS](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/blob/main/.github/CODEOWNERS) [(example)](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/commit/08f73efe826d5d47c0f93be5ed9e614740e82091)

7. **Create Pull Request**:
   - Reference the issue you created
   - Include tests in the `/tests` folder
   - Ensure all tests pass
   - Add sample prompts to `/e2eTests/e2eTestPrompts.md`
   - Follow code style requirements

## Testing

Command authors must provide both unit tests and end-to-end test prompts.

### Unit Tests

Unit tests live under the `/tests` folder. To run tests:

```pwsh
./eng/scripts/Test-Code.ps1
```

Requirements:
- Each command should have unit tests
- Tests should cover success and error scenarios
- Mock external service calls
- Test argument validation

### End-to-end Tests

End-to-end tests are performed manually. Command authors must thoroughly test each command to ensure correct tool invocation and results. At least one prompt per tool is required and should be added to `/e2eTests/e2eTestPrompts.md`.

### Testing Local Build with VS Code

To run the Azure MCP server from source for local development:

#### Build the Server

Navigate to the MCP server source directory and build the project:

```bash
dotnet build
```

#### Configure mcp.json

Update your mcp.json to point to the locally built azmcp executable:

```json
{
  "servers": {
    "azure-mcp-server": {
      "type": "stdio",
      "command": "<absolute-path-to>/azure-mcp/src/bin/Debug/net9.0/azmcp[.exe]",
      "args": ["server", "start"]
    }
  }
}
```

> **Note:** Replace `<absolute-path-to>` with the full path to your built executable.
> On **Windows**, use `azmcp.exe`. On **macOS/Linux**, use `azmcp`.

#### Server Modes

Optional `--namespace` and `--mode` parameters can be used to configure different server modes:

**Default Mode** (no additional parameters):
```json
{
  "servers": {
    "azure-mcp-server": {
      "type": "stdio",
      "command": "<absolute-path-to>/azure-mcp/src/bin/Debug/net9.0/azmcp[.exe]",
      "args": ["server", "start"]
    }
  }
}
```

**Namespace Mode** (expose specific services):
```json
{
  "servers": {
    "azure-mcp-server": {
      "type": "stdio",
      "command": "<absolute-path-to>/azure-mcp/src/bin/Debug/net9.0/azmcp[.exe]",
      "args": ["server", "start", "--namespace", "storage", "--namespace", "keyvault"]
    }
  }
}
```

**Namespace Proxy Mode** (collapse tools by namespace):
```json
{
  "servers": {
    "azure-mcp-server": {
      "type": "stdio",
      "command": "<absolute-path-to>/azure-mcp/src/bin/Debug/net9.0/azmcp[.exe]",
      "args": ["server", "start", "--mode", "namespace"]
    }
  }
}
```

**Single Tool Proxy Mode** (single "azure" tool with internal routing):
```json
{
  "servers": {
    "azure-mcp-server": {
      "type": "stdio",
      "command": "<absolute-path-to>/azure-mcp/src/bin/Debug/net9.0/azmcp[.exe]",
      "args": ["server", "start", "--mode", "single"]
    }
  }
}
```

**Combined Mode** (filter namespaces with proxy mode):
```json
{
  "servers": {
    "azure-mcp-server": {
      "type": "stdio",
      "command": "<absolute-path-to>/azure-mcp/src/bin/Debug/net9.0/azmcp[.exe]",
      "args": ["server", "start", "--namespace", "storage", "--namespace", "keyvault", "--mode", "namespace"]
    }
  }
}
```

> **Server Mode Summary:**
> - **Default Mode**: No additional parameters - exposes all tools individually
> - **Namespace Mode**: `--namespace <service-name>` - expose specific services
> - **Namespace Proxy Mode**: `--mode namespace` - collapse tools by namespace (useful for VS Code's 128 tool limit)
> - **Single Tool Mode**: `--mode single` - single "azure" tool with internal routing
> - **Combined Mode**: Both `--namespace` and `--mode` can be used together

#### Start from IDE

With the configuration in place, you can launch the MCP server directly from your IDE or any tooling that uses `mcp.json`.

### Live Tests

> ⚠️ If you are a Microsoft employee with Azure source permissions then please review our [Azure Internal Onboarding Documentation](https://aka.ms/azmcp/intake). Team members can run live tests by adding this comment to the PR `/azp run azure - mcp` to start the run.

Before running live tests:
- [Install Azure PowerShell](https://learn.microsoft.com/powershell/azure/install-azure-powershell)
- [Install Azure Bicep](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-resource-manager/bicep/install#install-manually)
- Login to Azure PowerShell: [`Connect-AzAccount`](https://learn.microsoft.com/powershell/azure/authenticate-interactive?view=azps-13.4.0)
- Deploy test resources:

```pwsh
./eng/scripts/Deploy-TestResources.ps1
```

**Deploy-TestResources.ps1 Parameters:**

| Parameter           | Type     | Description                                                                                                                   |
|---------------------|----------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `Areas`             | string[] | Reduce the scope of your deployment to specific areas. e.g. `-Areas Storage, KeyVault`                                       |
| `SubscriptionId`    | string   | Deploy to a specific subscription, otherwise, for internal users, the subscription will be defaulted to a known subscription. |
| `ResourceGroupName` | string   | Set the resource group name. Defaults to "{username}-mcp{hash(username)}".                                                    |
| `BaseName`          | string   | Set the base name for all of the resources. Defaults to "mcp{hash}".                                                          |
| `Unique`            | switch   | Make `{hash}` in the resource group name and base name unique per invocation. Defaults to a hash of your username             |
| `DeleteAfterHours`  | int      | Change the timespan used to set the DeleteAfter tag. Defaults to 12 hours.                                                   |

After deploying test resources, you should have a `.testsettings.json` file with your deployment information in the root of the repo.

Run live tests with:
```pwsh
./eng/scripts/Test-Code.ps1 -Live
```

You can scope tests to specific areas:
```pwsh
./eng/scripts/Test-Code.ps1 -Live -Areas Storage, KeyVault
```

### NPX Live Tests

You can set the `TestPackage` parameter in `.testsettings.json` to have live tests run `npx` targeting an arbitrary Azure MCP package:

```json
{
  "TenantId": "a20062a8-ff76-41c2-8a6d-5e843da7b051",
  "TenantName": "Your Tenant",
  "SubscriptionId": "cd27afdc-9976-4f08-96e9-cad120a91560",
  "SubscriptionName": "Your Subscription",
  "ResourceGroupName": "rg-abcdefg",
  "ResourceBaseName": "t1234567890",
  "TestPackage": "@azure/mcp@0.0.10"
}
```

To run live tests against the local build of an npm module:
```pwsh
./eng/scripts/Build-Local.ps1
```

This will produce .tgz files in the `.dist` directory and set the `TestPackage` parameter in the `.testsettings.json` file:

```json
"TestPackage": "file://D:\\repos\\azure-mcp\\.dist\\wrapper\\azure-mcp-0.0.12-alpha.1746488279.tgz"
```

### Debugging Live Tests

This section assumes that the necessary Azure resources for live tests are already deployed and that the `.testsettings.json` file with deployment information is located at the root of the local repository clone.

To debug the Azure MCP Server (`azmcp`) when running live tests in VS Code:

1. Build the package with debug symbols: `./eng/scripts/Build-Local.ps1 -DebugBuild`
2. Set a breakpoint in a command file (e.g., [`KeyValueListCommand.ExecuteAsync`](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/blob/4ed650a0507921273acc7b382a79049809ef39c1/src/Commands/AppConfig/KeyValue/KeyValueListCommand.cs#L48))
3. In VS Code, navigate to a test method (e.g., [`AppConfigCommandTests::Should_list_appconfig_kvs()`](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/blob/4ed650a0507921273acc7b382a79049809ef39c1/tests/Client/AppConfigCommandTests.cs#L56)), add a breakpoint to `CallToolAsync` call in the test method, then right-click and select **Debug Test**
4. Find the `azmcp` process ID:

```shell
pgrep -fl azmcp
```

```powershell
Get-Process | Where-Object { $_.ProcessName -like "*azmcp*" } | Select-Object Id, ProcessName, Path
```

5. Open the Command Palette (`Cmd+Shift+P` on Mac, `Ctrl+Shift+P` on Windows/Linux), select **Debug: Attach to .NET 5+ or .NET Core process**, and enter the `azmcp` process ID
6. Hit F5 to "Continue" debugging, the debugger should attach to `azmcp` and hit the breakpoint in command file

## Quality and Standards

### Code Style

To ensure consistent code quality, code format checks will run during all PR and CI builds. Run `dotnet format` before submitting to catch format errors early.

**Spelling Check**

To ensure consistent spelling across the codebase, run the spelling check before submitting:

```pwsh
.\eng\common\spelling\Invoke-Cspell.ps1
```

This will check all files for spelling errors using the project's dictionary. Add any new technical terms or proper nouns to `.vscode/cspell.json` if needed.

**Requirements:**
- Follow C# coding conventions
- No comments in implementation code (code should be self-documenting)
- Use descriptive variable and method names
- Follow the exact file structure and naming conventions
- Use proper error handling patterns
- XML documentation for public APIs
- Follow Model Context Protocol (MCP) patterns

### AOT Compatibility Analysis

The AOT compatibility analysis helps identify potential issues that might prevent the Azure MCP Server from working correctly when compiled with AOT or when trimming is enabled.

#### Running the Analysis

To run the AOT compatibility analysis locally:

```pwsh
./eng/scripts/Analyze-AOT-Compact.ps1
```

The HTML report will be generated at `.work/aotCompactReport/aot-compact-report.html` and automatically opened in your default browser.

To output the report to console, run the analysis with `-OutputFormat Console` argument.

AOT compatibility warnings typically indicate:
- Use of reflection without proper annotations
- Serialization of types that might be trimmed
- Dynamic code generation
- Use of `RequiresUnreferencedCodeAttribute` methods without proper precautions

#### Installing Git Hooks

You can install our pre-push hook to catch code format issues by automatically running `dotnet format` before each `git push`:

- `./eng/scripts/Install-GitHooks.ps1` - Installs the pre-push hook into your local repo
- `./eng/scripts/Remove-GitHooks.ps1` - Disables any git hooks in your local repo

### Model Context Protocol (MCP)

The Azure MCP Server implements the [Model Context Protocol specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io). When adding new commands:

- Follow MCP JSON schema patterns
- Implement proper context handling
- Use standardized response formats
- Handle errors according to MCP specifications
- Provide proper argument suggestions

## Advanced Configuration

### Configuring External MCP Servers

The Azure MCP Server supports connecting to external MCP servers through an embedded `registry.json` configuration file. This enables the server to act as a proxy, aggregating tools from multiple MCP servers into a single interface. The registry follows the same configuration schema as VS Code's `mcp.json`.

#### Registry Configuration

External MCP servers are defined in the embedded resource file `src/Areas/Server/Resources/registry.json`. This file contains server configurations that support both SSE (Server-Sent Events) and stdio transport mechanisms, following the standard MCP configuration format.

The registry structure follows this format:

```json
{
  "servers": {
    "documentation": {
      "url": "https://learn.microsoft.com/api/mcp",
      "description": "Search official Microsoft/Azure documentation..."
    },
    "another-server": {
      "type": "stdio",
      "command": "path/to/executable",
      "args": ["arg1", "arg2"],
      "env": {
        "ENV_VAR": "value"
      },
      "description": "Another MCP server using stdio transport"
    }
  }
}
```

#### Transport Types

**SSE (Server-Sent Events) Transport:**
- Use the `url` property to specify the endpoint
- Supports HTTP-based communication with automatic transport mode detection
- Best for web-based MCP servers and remote endpoints

**Stdio Transport:**
- Use `type: "stdio"` with the `command` property
- Supports launching external processes that communicate via standard input/output
- Use `args` array for command-line arguments
- Use `env` object for environment variables
- Best for local executables, command-line tools, and local MCP servers

#### Server Discovery and Namespace Filtering

External servers are automatically discovered when the Azure MCP Server starts. They can be filtered using the same namespace mechanisms as built-in commands:

```bash
# Include only specific external servers
azmcp server start --namespace documentation --namespace another-server

# Use namespace mode to group tools exposed by external servers
azmcp server start --mode namespace
```

#### Adding New External MCP Servers

To add a new external MCP server to the registry:

1. Edit `src/Areas/Server/Resources/registry.json`
2. Add your server configuration under the `servers` object using VS Code's MCP configuration schema
3. Use a unique identifier as the key
4. Provide either a `url` for SSE transport or `type: "stdio"` with `command` for stdio transport
5. Include a descriptive `description` field
6. Rebuild the project to embed the updated registry

#### Example External Servers

The current registry includes:
- **documentation**: Microsoft Learn documentation search via SSE transport
- Additional external servers can be added following the same pattern as VS Code's mcp.json

External servers integrate seamlessly with the Azure MCP Server's tool aggregation, appearing alongside native Azure commands in the unified tool interface. This allows you to combine local MCP servers, remote MCP endpoints, and Azure-specific tools in a single interface.

## Project Management

### Pull Request Process

1. Update documentation reflecting any changes
2. Add or update tests as needed
3. Reference the original issue
4. Wait for review and address any feedback

### Builds and Releases (Internal)

The internal pipeline [azure-mcp](https://dev.azure.com/azure-sdk/internal/_build?definitionId=7571) is used for all official releases and CI builds. On every merge to main, a build will run and will produce a dynamically named prerelease package on the public dev feed, e.g. [@azure/mcp@0.0.10-beta.4799791](https://dev.azure.com/azure-sdk/public/_artifacts/feed/azure-sdk-for-js/Npm/@azure%2Fmcp/overview/0.0.10-beta.4799791).

Only manual runs of the pipeline sign and publish packages. Building `main` or `hotfix/*` will publish to `npmjs.com`, all other refs will publish to the [public dev feed](https://dev.azure.com/azure-sdk/public/_artifacts/feed/azure-sdk-for-js).

Packages published to npmjs.com will always use the `@latest` [dist-tag](https://docs.npmjs.com/downloading-and-installing-packages-locally#installing-a-package-with-dist-tags).

Packages published to the dev feed will use:
- `@latest` for the latest official/release build
- `@dev` for the latest CI build of main
- `@pre` for any arbitrary pipeline run or feature branch build

#### PR Validation

To run live tests for a PR, inspect the PR code for any suspicious changes, then add the comment `/azp run azure - mcp` to the pull request. This will queue a PR triggered run which will build, run unit tests, deploy test resources and run live tests.

If you would like to see the product of a PR as a package on the dev feed, after thoroughly inspecting the change, create a branch in the main repo and manually trigger an [azure - mcp](https://dev.azure.com/azure-sdk/internal/_build?definitionId=7571) pipeline run against that branch. This will queue a manually triggered run which will build, run unit tests, deploy test resources, run live tests, sign and publish the packages to the dev feed.

Instructions for consuming the package from the dev feed can be found in the "Extensions" tab of the pipeline run page.

## Support and Community

### Questions and Support

We're building this in the open.  Your feedback is much appreciated, and will help us shape the future of the Azure MCP server.

👉 [Open an issue in the public repository](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/issues/new/choose).

### Additional Resources

- [Azure MCP Documentation](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/blob/main/README.md)
- [Command Implementation Guide](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/blob/main/src/Docs/new-command.md)
- [VS Code Insiders Download](https://code.visualstudio.com/insiders/)
- [GitHub Copilot Documentation](https://docs.github.com/en/copilot)

### Code of Conduct

This project has adopted the [Microsoft Open Source Code of Conduct](https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/). For more information see the [Code of Conduct FAQ](https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/faq/) or contact [opencode@microsoft.com](mailto:opencode@microsoft.com) with any additional questions or comments. By participating, you are expected to uphold this code.

### License

By contributing, you agree that your contributions will be licensed under the project's [license](https://github.com/Azure/azure-mcp/blob/main/LICENSE).

1→<!-- Copyright (c) Microsoft Corporation.
     2→<!-- Licensed under the MIT License. -->
     3→
     4→# Implementing a New Command in Azure MCP
     5→
     6→This document provides a comprehensive guide for implementing commands in Azure MCP following established patterns.
     7→
     8→## Area Pattern: Organizing Service Code
     9→
    10→All new services and their commands should use the Area pattern:
    11→
    12→- **Service code** goes in `src/Areas/{ServiceName}` (e.g., `src/Areas/Storage`)
    13→- **Tests** go in `tests/Areas/{ServiceName}`
    14→
    15→This keeps all code, options, models, and tests for a service together. See `src/Areas/Storage` for a reference implementation.
    16→
    17→## Command Architecture
    18→
    19→### Command Design Principles
    20→
    21→1. **Command Interface**
    22→   - `IBaseCommand` serves as the root interface with core command capabilities:
    23→     - `Name`: Command name for CLI display
    24→     - `Description`: Detailed command description
    25→     - `Title`: Human-readable command title
    26→     - `GetCommand()`: Retrieves System.CommandLine command definition
    27→     - `ExecuteAsync()`: Executes command logic
    28→     - `Validate()`: Validates command inputs
    29→
    30→2. **Command Hierarchy**
    31→   All commands must implement the hierarchy pattern:
    32→     ```
    33→     IBaseCommand
    34→     └── BaseCommand
    35→         └── GlobalCommand<TOptions>
    36→             └── SubscriptionCommand<TOptions>
    37→                 └── Service-specific base commands (e.g., BaseSqlCommand)
    38→                     └── Resource-specific commands (e.g., SqlIndexRecommendCommand)
    39→     ```
    40→
    41→   IMPORTANT:
    42→   - Commands use primary constructors with ILogger injection
    43→   - Classes are always sealed unless explicitly intended for inheritance
    44→   - Commands inheriting from SubscriptionCommand must handle subscription parameters
    45→   - Service-specific base commands should add service-wide options
    46→   - Commands are marked with [McpServerTool] attribute to define their characteristics
    47→
    48→3. **Command Pattern**
    49→   Commands follow the Model-Context-Protocol (MCP) pattern with this naming convention:
    50→   ```
    51→   azmcp <azure service> <resource> <operation>
    52→   ```
    53→   Example: `azmcp storage container list`
    54→
    55→   Where:
    56→   - `azure service`: Azure service name (lowercase, e.g., storage, cosmos, kusto)
    57→   - `resource`: Resource type (singular noun, lowercase)
    58→   - `operation`: Action to perform (verb, lowercase)
    59→
    60→   Each command is:
    61→   - In code, to avoid ambiguity between service classes and Azure services, we refer to Azure services as Areas
    62→   - Registered in the RegisterCommands method of its service's Areas/{Area}/{Area}Setup.cs file
    63→   - Organized in a hierarchy of command groups
    64→   - Documented with a title, description and examples
    65→   - Validated before execution
    66→   - Returns a standardized response format
    67→
    68→   **IMPORTANT**: Command group names cannot contain underscores. Use camelCase or concatenated names or dash separator instead:
    69→   - ✅ Good: `new CommandGroup("entraadmin", "Entra admin operations")`
    70→   - ✅ Good: `new CommandGroup("resourcegroup", "Resource group operations")`
    71→   - ✅ Good:`new CommandGroup("entra-admin", "Entra admin operations")`
    72→   - ❌ Bad: `new CommandGroup("entra_admin", "Entra admin operations")`
    73→
    74→### Required Files
    75→
    76→A complete command requires:
    77→
    78→1. OptionDefinitions static class: `src/Areas/{Area}/Options/{Area}OptionDefinitions.cs`
    79→2. Options class: `src/Areas/{Area}/Options/{Resource}/{Operation}Options.cs`
    80→3. Command class: `src/Areas/{Area}/Commands/{Resource}/{Resource}{Operation}Command.cs`
    81→4. Service interface: `src/Areas/{Area}/Services/I{Service}Service.cs`
    82→5. Service implementation: `src/Areas/{Area}/Services/{Service}Service.cs`
    83→   - {Area} and {Service} should not be considered synonymous
    84→   - It's common for an area to have a single service class named after the
    85→     area but some areas will have multiple service classes
    86→6. Unit test: `tests/Areas/{Area}/UnitTests/{Resource}/{Resource}{Operation}CommandTests.cs`
    87→7. Integration test: `tests/Areas/{Area}/LiveTests/{Area}CommandTests.cs`
    88→8. Command registration in RegisterCommands(): `src/Areas/{Area}/{Area}Setup.cs`
    89→9. Area registration in RegisterAreas(): `src/Program.cs`
    90→10. **Live test infrastructure** (if needed):
    91→   - Bicep template: `/infra/services/{area}.bicep`
    92→   - Module registration in: `/infra/test-resources.bicep`
    93→   - Optional post-deployment script: `/infra/services/{area}-post.ps1`
    94→
    95→**IMPORTANT**: If implementing a new area, you must also ensure:
    96→- The Azure Resource Manager package is added to `Directory.Packages.props` first
    97→- The package reference is added to `src/AzureMcp.csproj`
    98→- Models, base commands, and option definitions follow the established patterns
    99→- JSON serialization context includes all new model types
   100→- Service registration in the area setup ConfigureServices method
   101→- **Live test infrastructure**: Add Bicep template to `/infra/services/` and module to `/infra/test-resources.bicep`
   102→- **Test resource deployment**: Ensure resources are properly configured with RBAC for test application
   103→- **Resource naming**: Follow consistent naming patterns - many services use just `baseName`, while others may need suffixes for disambiguation (e.g., `{baseName}-suffix`)
   104→
   105→## Implementation Guidelines
   106→
   107→### 1. Azure Resource Manager Integration
   108→
   109→When creating commands that interact with Azure services, you'll need to:
   110→
   111→**Package Management:**
   112→- Add the appropriate Azure Resource Manager package to both `Directory.Packages.props` and `AzureMcp.csproj`
   113→- Example: `<PackageVersion Include="Azure.ResourceManager.Sql" Version="1.3.0" />`
   114→
   115→**Subscription Resolution:**
   116→- Always use `ISubscriptionService.GetSubscription()` to resolve subscription ID or name
   117→- Inject `ISubscriptionService` into your service constructor
   118→- This handles both subscription IDs and subscription names automatically
   119→- Example pattern:
   120→```csharp
   121→public class MyService(ISubscriptionService subscriptionService, ITenantService tenantService) 
   122→    : BaseAzureService(tenantService), IMyService
   123→{
   124→    private readonly ISubscriptionService _subscriptionService = subscriptionService;
   125→    
   126→    public async Task<MyResource> GetResourceAsync(string subscription, ...)
   127→    {
   128→        var subscriptionResource = await _subscriptionService.GetSubscription(subscription, null, retryPolicy);
   129→        // Use subscriptionResource instead of creating one manually
   130→    }
   131→}
   132→```
   133→
   134→**API Pattern Discovery:**
   135→- Study existing services (e.g., Postgres, Redis) to understand resource access patterns
   136→- Use resource collections correctly: `.GetSqlServers().GetAsync(serverName)` not `.GetSqlServerAsync(serverName, cancellationToken)`
   137→- Check Azure SDK documentation for correct method signatures and property names
   138→
   139→**Common Azure Resource Manager Patterns:**
   140→```csharp
   141→// Correct pattern for subscription resolution and resource access
   142→var subscriptionResource = await _subscriptionService.GetSubscription(subscription, tenant, retryPolicy);
   143→
   144→var resourceGroupResource = await subscriptionResource
   145→    .GetResourceGroupAsync(resourceGroup, cancellationToken);
   146→
   147→var sqlServerResource = await resourceGroupResource.Value
   148→    .GetSqlServers()
   149→    .GetAsync(serverName);
   150→
   151→var databaseResource = await sqlServerResource.Value
   152→    .GetSqlDatabases()
   153→    .GetAsync(databaseName);
   154→```
   155→
   156→**Property Access Issues:**
   157→- Azure SDK property names may differ from expected names (e.g., `CreatedOn` not `CreationDate`)
   158→- Check actual property availability using IntelliSense or SDK documentation
   159→- Some properties are objects that need `.ToString()` conversion (e.g., `Location.ToString()`)
   160→- Be aware of nullable properties and use appropriate null checks
   161→
   162→**Compilation Error Resolution:**
   163→- When you see `cannot convert from 'System.Threading.CancellationToken' to 'string'`, check method parameter order
   164→- For `'SqlDatabaseData' does not contain a definition for 'X'`, verify property names in the actual SDK types
   165→- Use existing service implementations as reference for correct property access patterns
   166→
   167→### 2. Options Class
   168→
   169→```csharp
   170→public class {Resource}{Operation}Options : Base{Service}Options
   171→{
   172→    // Only add properties not in base class
   173→    public string? NewOption { get; set; }
   174→}
   175→```
   176→
   177→IMPORTANT:
   178→- Inherit from appropriate base class (BaseServiceOptions, GlobalOptions, etc.)
   179→- Never redefine properties from base classes
   180→- Make properties nullable if not required
   181→- Use consistent parameter names across services:
   182→  - **CRITICAL**: Always use `subscription` (never `subscriptionId`) for subscription parameters - this allows the parameter to accept both subscription IDs and subscription names, which are resolved internally by `ISubscriptionService.GetSubscription()`
   183→  - Use `resourceGroup` instead of `resourceGroupName`
   184→  - Use singular nouns for resource names (e.g., `server` not `serverName`)
   185→  - Keep parameter names consistent with Azure SDK parameters when possible
   186→  - If services share similar operations (e.g., ListDatabases), use the same parameter order and names
   187→
   188→### 2. Command Class
   189→
   190→```csharp
   191→public sealed class {Resource}{Operation}Command(ILogger<{Resource}{Operation}Command> logger)
   192→    : Base{Service}Command<{Resource}{Operation}Options>
   193→{
   194→    private const string CommandTitle = "Human Readable Title";
   195→    private readonly ILogger<{Resource}{Operation}Command> _logger = logger;
   196→
   197→    // Define options from OptionDefinitions
   198→    private readonly Option<string> _newOption = {Area}OptionDefinitions.NewOption;
   199→
   200→    public override string Name => "operation";
   201→
   202→    public override string Description =>
   203→        """
   204→        Detailed description of what the command does.
   205→        Returns description of return format.
   206→          Required options:
   207→        - list required options
   208→        """;
   209→
   210→    public override string Title => CommandTitle;
   211→
   212→    protected override void RegisterOptions(Command command)
   213→    {
   214→        base.RegisterOptions(command);
   215→        command.AddOption(_newOption);
   216→    }
   217→
   218→    protected override {Resource}{Operation}Options BindOptions(ParseResult parseResult)
   219→    {
   220→        var options = base.BindOptions(parseResult);
   221→        options.NewOption = parseResult.GetValueForOption(_newOption);
   222→        return options;
   223→    }
   224→
   225→    [McpServerTool(
   226→        Destructive = false,     // Set to true for commands that modify resources
   227→        ReadOnly = true,        // Set to false for commands that modify resources
   228→        Title = CommandTitle)]  // Display name shown in UI
   229→    public override async Task<CommandResponse> ExecuteAsync(CommandContext context, ParseResult parseResult)
   230→    {
   231→        var options = BindOptions(parseResult);
   232→
   233→        try
   234→        {
   235→            // Required validation step
   236→            if (!Validate(parseResult.CommandResult, context.Response).IsValid)
   237→            {
   238→                return context.Response;
   239→            }
   240→
   241→            AddSubscriptionInformation(context.Activity, options); 
   242→
   243→            // Get the appropriate service from DI
   244→            var service = context.GetService<I{Service}Service>();
   245→
   246→            // Call service operation(s) with required parameters
   247→            var results = await service.{Operation}(
   248→                options.RequiredParam!,  // Required parameters end with !
   249→                options.OptionalParam,   // Optional parameters are nullable
   250→                options.Subscription!,   // From SubscriptionCommand
   251→                options.RetryPolicy);    // From GlobalCommand
   252→
   253→            // Set results if any were returned
   254→            context.Response.Results = results?.Count > 0 ?
   255→                ResponseResult.Create(
   256→                    new {Operation}CommandResult(results),
   257→                    {Service}JsonContext.Default.{Operation}CommandResult) :
   258→                null;
   259→        }
   260→        catch (Exception ex)
   261→        {
   262→            // Log error with all relevant context
   263→            _logger.LogError(ex,
   264→                "Error in {Operation}. Required: {Required}, Optional: {Optional}, Options: {@Options}",
   265→                Name, options.RequiredParam, options.OptionalParam, options);
   266→            HandleException(context.Response, ex);
   267→        }
   268→
   269→        return context.Response;
   270→    }
   271→
   272→    // Implementation-specific error handling
   273→    protected override string GetErrorMessage(Exception ex) => ex switch
   274→    {
   275→        Azure.RequestFailedException reqEx when reqEx.Status == 404 =>
   276→            "Resource not found. Verify the resource exists and you have access.",
   277→        Azure.RequestFailedException reqEx when reqEx.Status == 403 =>
   278→            $"Authorization failed accessing the resource. Details: {reqEx.Message}",
   279→        Azure.RequestFailedException reqEx => reqEx.Message,
   280→        _ => base.GetErrorMessage(ex)
   281→    };
   282→
   283→    protected override int GetStatusCode(Exception ex) => ex switch
   284→    {
   285→        Azure.RequestFailedException reqEx => reqEx.Status,
   286→        _ => base.GetStatusCode(ex)
   287→    };
   288→
   289→    // Strongly-typed result records
   290→    internal record {Resource}{Operation}CommandResult(List<ResultType> Results);
   291→}
   292→
   293→### 3. Base Service Command Classes
   294→
   295→Each service has its own hierarchy of base command classes that inherit from `GlobalCommand` or `SubscriptionCommand`. Services that work with Azure resources should inject `ISubscriptionService` for subscription resolution. For example:
   296→
   297→```csharp
   298→// Copyright (c) Microsoft Corporation.
   299→// Licensed under the MIT License.
   300→
   301→using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;
   302→using AzureMcp.Commands.Subscription;
   303→using AzureMcp.Models.Option;
   304→using AzureMcp.Options.{Service};
   305→using Azure.Core;
   306→using AzureMcp.Models;
   307→using Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;
   308→
   309→namespace AzureMcp.Commands.{Service};
   310→
   311→// Base command for all service commands (if no members needed, use concise syntax)
   312→public abstract class Base{Service}Command<
   313→    [DynamicallyAccessedMembers(TrimAnnotations.CommandAnnotations)] TOptions>
   314→    : SubscriptionCommand<TOptions> where TOptions : Base{Service}Options, new();
   315→
   316→// Base command for all service commands (if members are needed, use full syntax)
   317→public abstract class Base{Service}Command<
   318→    [DynamicallyAccessedMembers(TrimAnnotations.CommandAnnotations)] TOptions>
   319→    : SubscriptionCommand<TOptions> where TOptions : Base{Service}Options, new()
   320→{
   321→    protected readonly Option<string> _commonOption = {Area}OptionDefinitions.CommonOption;
   322→    protected readonly Option<string> _resourceGroupOption = OptionDefinitions.Common.ResourceGroup;
   323→    protected virtual bool RequiresResourceGroup => true;
   324→
   325→    protected override void RegisterOptions(Command command)
   326→    {
   327→        base.RegisterOptions(command);
   328→        command.AddOption(_commonOption);
   329→
   330→        // Add resource group option if required
   331→        if (RequiresResourceGroup)
   332→        {
   333→            command.AddOption(_resourceGroupOption);
   334→        }
   335→    }
   336→
   337→    protected override TOptions BindOptions(ParseResult parseResult)
   338→    {
   339→        var options = base.BindOptions(parseResult);
   340→        options.CommonOption = parseResult.GetValueForOption(_commonOption);
   341→
   342→        if (RequiresResourceGroup)
   343→        {
   344→            options.ResourceGroup = parseResult.GetValueForOption(_resourceGroupOption);
   345→        }
   346→
   347→        return options;
   348→    }
   349→}
   350→
   351→// Service implementation example with subscription resolution
   352→public class {Service}Service(ISubscriptionService subscriptionService, ITenantService tenantService) 
   353→    : BaseAzureService(tenantService), I{Service}Service
   354→{
   355→    private readonly ISubscriptionService _subscriptionService = subscriptionService ?? throw new ArgumentNullException(nameof(subscriptionService));
   356→
   357→    public async Task<{Resource}> GetResourceAsync(string subscription, string resourceGroup, string resourceName, RetryPolicyOptions? retryPolicy)
   358→    {
   359→        // Always use subscription service for resolution
   360→        var subscriptionResource = await _subscriptionService.GetSubscription(subscription, null, retryPolicy);
   361→        
   362→        var resourceGroupResource = await subscriptionResource
   363→            .GetResourceGroupAsync(resourceGroup, cancellationToken);
   364→        // Continue with resource access...
   365→    }
   366→}
   367→```

   369→### 4. Unit Tests
   370→
   371→Unit tests follow a standardized pattern that tests initialization, validation, and execution:
   372→
   373→```csharp
   374→public class {Resource}{Operation}CommandTests
   375→{
   376→    private readonly IServiceProvider _serviceProvider;
   377→    private readonly I{Service}Service _service;
   378→    private readonly ILogger<{Resource}{Operation}Command> _logger;
   379→    private readonly {Resource}{Operation}Command _command;
   380→
   381→    public {Resource}{Operation}CommandTests()
   382→    {
   383→        _service = Substitute.For<I{Service}Service>();
   384→        _logger = Substitute.For<ILogger<{Resource}{Operation}Command>>();
   385→
   386→        var collection = new ServiceCollection();
   387→        collection.AddSingleton(_service);
   388→        _serviceProvider = collection.BuildServiceProvider();
   389→
   390→        _command = new(_logger);
   391→    }
   392→
   393→    [Fact]
   394→    public void Constructor_InitializesCommandCorrectly()
   395→    {
   396→        var command = _command.GetCommand();
   397→        Assert.Equal("operation", command.Name);
   398→        Assert.NotNull(command.Description);
   399→        Assert.NotEmpty(command.Description);
   400→    }
   401→
   402→    [Theory]
   403→    [InlineData("--required value", true)]
   404→    [InlineData("--optional-param value --required value", true)]
   405→    [InlineData("", false)]
   406→    public async Task ExecuteAsync_ValidatesInputCorrectly(string args, bool shouldSucceed)
   407→    {
   408→        // Arrange
   409→        if (shouldSucceed)
   410→        {
   411→            _service.{Operation}(Arg.Any<{Resource}{Operation}Options>())
   412→                .Returns(new List<ResultType>());
   413→        }
   414→
   415→        var context = new CommandContext(_serviceProvider);
   416→        var parseResult = _command.GetCommand().Parse(args);
   417→
   418→        // Act
   419→        var response = await _command.ExecuteAsync(context, parseResult);
   420→
   421→        // Assert
   422→        Assert.Equal(shouldSucceed ? 200 : 400, response.Status);
   423→        if (shouldSucceed)
   424→        {
   425→            Assert.NotNull(response.Results);
   426→            Assert.Equal("Success", response.Message);
   427→        }
   428→        else
   429→        {
   430→            Assert.Contains("required", response.Message.ToLower());
   431→        }
   432→    }
   433→
   434→    [Fact]
   435→    public async Task ExecuteAsync_HandlesServiceErrors()
   436→    {
   437→        // Arrange
   438→        _service.{Operation}(Arg.Any<{Resource}{Operation}Options>())
   439→            .Returns(Task.FromException<List<ResultType>>(new Exception("Test error")));
   440→
   441→        var context = new CommandContext(_serviceProvider);
   442→        var parseResult = _command.GetCommand().Parse("--required value");
   443→
   444→        // Act
   445→        var response = await _command.ExecuteAsync(context, parseResult);
   446→
   447→        // Assert
   448→        Assert.Equal(500, response.Status);
   449→        Assert.Contains("Test error", response.Message);
   450→        Assert.Contains("troubleshooting", response.Message);
   451→    }
   452→}

   556→## Error Handling
   557→
   558→Commands in Azure MCP follow a standardized error handling approach using the base `HandleException` method inherited from `BaseCommand`. Here are the key aspects:
   559→
   560→### 1. Status Code Mapping
   561→The base implementation handles common status codes:
   562→```csharp
   563→protected virtual int GetStatusCode(Exception ex) => ex switch
   564→{
   565→    // Common error response codes
   566→    AuthenticationFailedException => 401,   // Unauthorized
   567→    RequestFailedException rfEx => rfEx.Status,  // Service-reported status
   568→    HttpRequestException => 503,   // Service unavailable
   569→    ValidationException => 400,    // Bad request
   570→    _ => 500  // Unknown errors
   571→};
   572→```
   573→
   574→### 2. Error Message Formatting
   575→Error messages should be user-actionable and help debug issues:
   576→```csharp
   577→protected virtual string GetErrorMessage(Exception ex) => ex switch
   578→{
   579→    AuthenticationFailedException authEx =>
   580→        $"Authentication failed. Please run 'az login' to sign in. Details: {authEx.Message}",
   581→    RequestFailedException rfEx => rfEx.Message,
   582→    HttpRequestException httpEx =>
   583→        $"Service unavailable or connectivity issues. Details: {httpEx.Message}",
   584→    _ => ex.Message
   585→};
   586→```
   587→
   588→### 3. Response Format
   589→The base `HandleException` combines status, message and details:
   590→```csharp
   591→protected virtual void HandleException(CommandResponse response, Exception ex)
   592→{
   593→    // Create a strongly typed exception result
   594→    var result = new ExceptionResult(
   595→        Message: ex.Message,
   596→        StackTrace: ex.StackTrace,
   597→        Type: ex.GetType().Name);
   598→
   599→    response.Status = GetStatusCode(ex);
   600→    // Add link to troubleshooting guide
   601→    response.Message = GetErrorMessage(ex) +
   602→        ". Details at https://aka.ms/azmcp/troubleshooting";
   603→    response.Results = ResponseResult.Create(
   604→        result, JsonSourceGenerationContext.Default.ExceptionResult);
   605→}
   606→```
   607→
   608→### 4. Service-Specific Errors
   609→Commands should override error handlers to add service-specific mappings:
   610→```csharp
   611→protected override string GetErrorMessage(Exception ex) => ex switch
   612→{
   613→    // Add service-specific cases
   614→    ResourceNotFoundException =>
   615→        "Resource not found. Verify name and permissions.",
   616→    ServiceQuotaExceededException =>
   617→        "Service quota exceeded. Request quota increase.",
   618→    _ => base.GetErrorMessage(ex) // Fall back to base implementation
   619→};
   620→```
   621→
   622→### 5. Error Context Logging
   623→Always log errors with relevant context information:
   624→```csharp
   625→catch (Exception ex)
   626→{
   627→    _logger.LogError(ex,
   628→        "Error in {Operation}. Resource: {Resource}, Options: {@Options}",
   629→        Name, resourceId, options);
   630→    HandleException(context.Response, ex);
   631→}
   632→```
   633→
   634→### 6. Common Error Scenarios to Handle
   635→
   636→1. **Authentication/Authorization**
   637→   - Azure credential expiry
   638→   - Missing RBAC permissions
   639→   - Invalid connection strings
   640→
   641→2. **Validation**
   642→   - Missing required parameters
   643→   - Invalid parameter formats
   644→   - Conflicting options
   645→
   646→3. **Resource State**
   647→   - Resource not found
   648→   - Resource locked/in use
   649→   - Invalid resource state
   650→
   651→4. **Service Limits**
   652→   - Throttling/rate limits
   653→   - Quota exceeded
   654→   - Service capacity
   655→
   656→5. **Network/Connectivity**
   657→   - Service unavailable
   658→   - Request timeouts
   659→   - Network failures
   660→

   662→
   663→### Unit Tests
   664→Core test cases for every command:
   665→```csharp
   666→[Theory]
   667→[InlineData("", false, "Missing required options")]  // Validation
   668→[InlineData("--param invalid", false, "Invalid format")] // Input format
   669→[InlineData("--param value", true, null)]  // Success case
   670→public async Task ExecuteAsync_ValidatesInput(
   671→    string args, bool shouldSucceed, string expectedError)
   672→{
